coredata.dc:title: Size-dependent antibacterial of carbon dots by selective absorption and differential oxidative stress of bacteria
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Du, Xin
Zhang, Mengling
Ma, Yurong
Wang, Xiting
Liu, Yang
Huang, Hui
Kang, Zhenhui
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs), as one kind of zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterials, show great potential in combating emerging infectious diseases and antimicrobial infections. CDs with outstanding optical properties and benign biocompatibility have been reported as excellent antibacterial agents. However, few reports were focused on the relationship between the CDs’ size and their antibacterial activity. Herein, the desired CDs (VCDs) were fabricated by a one-step electrochemical oxidation method using l-ascorbic acid as raw material, and four types of VCDs with different sizes were obtained by adjusting the reaction times. The effectiveness of antibacterial activity demonstrates the VCDs display size-dependent antibacterial activity, where the VCDs-2 (average size: 2.92 nm) exhibit superior antibacterial activity to others, attributing to the synergy of the absorption capacity of bacteria to VCDs and the ROS stimulated by VCDs. The VCDs-2 could more easily penetrate bacterial cells, stimulate the production of ROS, damage the cell walls of E. coli, and inhibit the growth and reproduction of bacteria. This work helps to understand the effect of CDs’ size on antibacterial properties, and provides a direction for the design of novel antimicrobials with drug resistance.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Size-dependent
Antibacterial activity
Reactive oxygen species

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The relative content of hydroxyls versus carboxyls

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis, morphology and particle size characterization of VCDs. (a) Schematic fabrication of VCDs-1, VCDs-2, VCDs-3 and VCDs-4 by a one-step electrochemical oxidation method. (b)-(e) TEM images and particle size distributions (insert) of VCDs-1, VCDs-2, VCDs-3 and VCDs-4. (f)-(i) DLS of VCDs-1, VCDs-2, VCDs-3 and VCDs-4.

Structural characterizations of VCDs. (a) Atom ratios of carbon and oxygen of VCDs-1, VCDs-2, VCDs-3 and VCDs-4. (b) High-resolution C 1s XPS spectra of VCDs-1, VCDs-2, VCDs-3 and VCDs-4. (c) FT-IR spectra of l-ascorbic acid (black line), VCDs-1 (red line), VCDs-2 (blue line), VCDs-3 (green line) and VCDs-4 (purple line). (d) The relative content of hydroxyl vs. carboxyl for VCDs-1, VCDs-2, VCDs-3 and VCDs-4 (the results were presented as mean ± standard deviation).

(a) Digital photographs of E. coli colony formation after the treatment with VCDs-1, VCDs-2, VCDs-3 and VCDs-4 (400 μg·mL−1) for 24 h. (b) Colony counts after the treatment with VCDs-1, VCDs-2, VCDs-3 and VCDs-4 (400 μg·mL−1) for 24 h. (c) Bacterial viability was evaluated by OD600, and the E. coli were treated with VCDs-1, VCDs-2, VCDs-3 and VCDs-4 (400 μg·mL−1), respectively.

(a) Digital photographs of E. coli colonies treated with different concentrations of VCDs-2 (0, 200, 400, 600, 800 and 1000 μg·mL−1) for 24 h. (b) Antibacterial rate of E. coli treated with l-ascorbic acid and VCDs-2 by testing the OD600 through UV–Vis spectroscopy. (b) Antibacterial rate of E. coli treated with different oxidation degree of VCDs-2 (400 μg·mL−1) by testing the OD600 through UV–Vis spectroscopy.

(a) The ROS contents of E. coli treated without and with VCDs-1, VCDs-2, VCDs-3 and VCDs-4 (200 μg·mL−1). (b-c) SEM images of E. coli treated without and with VCDs-2 (200 μg·mL−1). (d-e) The CLSM images of E. coli were treated without and with VCDs-2. (λ ex = 405 nm; emission peak was collected at 415–550 nm).
Schematic diagram of the antimicrobial effects of VCDs.

head.ce:title: Size-dependent antibacterial of carbon dots by selective absorption and differential oxidative stress of bacteria

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Institute of Functional Nano and Soft Materials Laboratory (FUNSOM), Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices, Soochow University, Suzhou 215123, Jiangsu, PR China (H. Huang).

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Functional Nano and Soft Materials Laboratory (FUNSOM)
Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The effective size-dependent antibacterial activity for the VCDs should attribute to the synergy of the absorption capacity of bacteria to VCDs and the ROS stimulated by VCDs.

Carbon dots (CDs), as one kind of zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterials, show great potential in combating emerging infectious diseases and antimicrobial infections. CDs with outstanding optical properties and benign biocompatibility have been reported as excellent antibacterial agents. However, few reports were focused on the relationship between the CDs’ size and their antibacterial activity. Herein, the desired CDs (VCDs) were fabricated by a one-step electrochemical oxidation method using l-ascorbic acid as raw material, and four types of VCDs with different sizes were obtained by adjusting the reaction times. The effectiveness of antibacterial activity demonstrates the VCDs display size-dependent antibacterial activity, where the VCDs-2 (average size: 2.92 nm) exhibit superior antibacterial activity to others, attributing to the synergy of the absorption capacity of bacteria to VCDs and the ROS stimulated by VCDs. The VCDs-2 could more easily penetrate bacterial cells, stimulate the production of ROS, damage the cell walls of E. coli, and inhibit the growth and reproduction of bacteria. This work helps to understand the effect of CDs’ size on antibacterial properties, and provides a direction for the design of novel antimicrobials with drug resistance.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Human bacterial infection remains a growing global health problem, with the most widely accepted treatment limited to antibiotics [1,2]. Even though some effective antibiotics could significantly reduce mortality from bacterial infections, the overuse and misuse of these antibiotics contribute to the rapid evolution and spread of antibiotic resistance, making the treatment of bacterial infections more and more difficult [3,4]. Nanomaterials are increasingly being used to treat diseases caused by bacteria and have shown great potential. The size effects of nanoparticles in terms of large surface area, broadband gap, and edge site exhibit unusual behavior in all aspects of catalytic activity, optics, cell viability, protein folding, and aggregation [5–7]. At the same time, there is increasing evidence that the size of materials also plays an important role in determining antibacterial activity, but most materials involve metal nanoparticles [8,9], metal copolymers [10,11] and metal oxide particles [12,13]. Whereas, there is little literature to study the size effect of metal-free carbon based nanomaterials on antibacterial activity, especially their size is less than 10 nm. To this end, exploring the relationship between the pony-size of nanomaterials and antimicrobial activity is an urgent challenge.

Carbon dots (CDs), as one kind of zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterials with a diameter of less than 10 nm, show great potential in combating emerging infectious diseases and antimicrobial infections due to their outstanding properties, such as benign biocompatibility, low cost, and so on [14–17]. At present, a variety of CDs have been reported to be used as antibacterial agents for the treatment of antibacterial infections. The various CDs synthesized from different precursors could exhibit the characteristics such as selective antimicrobial [18,19], photodynamic antibacterial [20–23], or broad-spectrum antibacterial [24–26]. In addition, these studies have shown that the properties of CDs, such as functional groups [27,28] and the surface charge would affect their antimicrobial properties [29,30]. The size of the CDs also greatly determines the antibacterial performance. Based on our knowledge, only Sun et al. [31] have separated the CDs derived from chlorhexidine gluconate into three groups with uniformly small-scale, middle-scale, and large-scale particle sizes by using different molecular weight cut-off membranes with hydrothermal method for antibiosis. They are namely, ∼2.0 nm small particle size, ∼3.9 nm middle particle size, and ∼5.3 nm large particle size. They found that the small size CDs (∼2 nm) are easier to cross the lipid bilayer and interact with DNA molecules, showing stronger antimicrobial activity. However, whether the CDs with smaller size (<2 nm) have superior antibacterial performance and antibacterial mechanism are still unclear.

Herein, four kinds of CDs with different sizes (VCDs-1 ∼ VCDs-4, average size: 1.27, 2.92, 3.56 and 4.41 nm) were fabricated by one-step electrochemical oxidation method from the l-ascorbic acid with different reaction times. The antibacterial activity and antibacterial mechanism of these VCDs were systematically analyzed. As the particle size of VCDs increases, the antimicrobial activity increases first and then decreases. Smaller size of VCDs could more easily absorbed by the bacteria via free diffusion, but the too small VCDs-1 (Average size = 1.27 nm) would not stimulate the bacteria to produce excess ROS, for which they show the weakest antimicrobial activity. When the size is large enough (2–5 nm), the antibacterial properties decrease with increasing size. Serial characterizations show the VCDs can penetrate bacterial cells, induce cellular oxidative stress caused by the generation of ROS, damage the cell walls, and inhibit bacterial growth and reproduction. The effectiveness of size-dependent antimicrobial activity for VCDs should attribute to the synergistic effect of bacterial absorption of VCDs and ROS stimulated by VCDs. We further confirmed and proposed the effectiveness of size-dependent antimicrobial activity for VCDs should attribute to the synergistic effect of bacterial absorption of VCDs and ROS stimulated by VCDs.

The chemicals used in this work were purchased from Shanghai Yuanye Biotechnology, Shanghai Sinopharm Reagent Co. Ltd. and Sigma-Aldrich Co. Ltd. The l-ascorbic acid and ethanol used in the experiment are all analytial reagent (AR). The platinum wire (>99.95%, 0.5 mm) was purchased from Shanghai Sinopharm Reagent Co. Ltd. Reactive oxygen species (ROS) assay kit was ordered from G-CLONE Reagent Co. Ltd. The cell counting kit-8 (CCK-8) was purchased from Beyotime. All chemicals were used without any further purification. Milli-Q ultrapure water was used for all experiments.

The morphology and size of VCDs were characterized with an FEI TF20 transmission electron microscope (TEM.) The morphology of the bacteria was obtained using a Zeiss G500 scanning electron microscope (SEM). The particle sizes of the VCDs were measured by the dynamic light scattering (DLS) instrument. The KRATOS Axis ultra-DLD X-ray photoelectron spectrometer was employed to obtain the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectrum. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum and the ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectrum were obtained using a Hyperion FT-IR spectrometer and a PE750 ultraviolet–visible spectrometer, respectively. Conductometric titration was performed using a DDS-11A conductance titrator. The confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) images were obtained by a laser-scanning confocal fluorescence microscope (Leica, TCS-SP5).

The VCDs were fabricated by a one-step electrochemical oxidation method using l-ascorbic acid as raw material. Briefly, 10 g l-ascorbic acid was added in 400 mL ultrapure water and stirred for 20 min until the solid powder of l-ascorbic acid was completely dissolved. Then, two platinum wires (diameter: 0.5 mm) were used as anode and cathode and inserted into the l-ascorbic acid solution. The work current is 0.3 A during the reaction. Samples were taken on days 3, 7, 11 and 15, respectively. The obtained products were dialyzed via a 500–1000 Da dialysis bag to remove excess l-ascorbic acid. Finally, four kinds of VCDs solutions (VCDs-1, VCDs-2, VCDs-3 and VCDs-4) with different sizes were obtained. The obtained VCDs solutions were stored at 4 ℃ for further experiments.

The 293 T cell line was used for evaluating the cytotoxicity of VCDs [32], which was cultured in a standard medium with 5% CO2 at 37 ℃. Firstly, 293 T cells were transferred to cultivate in a 96-well plate (1 × 104 cells per well) for 24 h. After 24 h, the fresh medium with different concentrations of VCDs (0, 200, 400, 600, 800 μg·mL−1) was replaced with the original medium and the cells were cultured for another 24 h. Then, the cell viability treated with different concentrations of VCDs was measured by CCK-8. All measurements were done in triplicate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The relative content of hydroxyls versus carboxyls
ce:section.ce:para:
Conductivity titration was used to determine the relative content of hydroxyl and carboxyl on the surface of VCDs [33,34]. VCDs solution (1 mL, 100 mg·mL−1), absolute ethanol (1 mL) and the solvent (acetone/pyridine = 4/1, 40 mL) were mixed in a 50 mL three-neck flask. The mixture was thoroughly stirred at room temperature for 10 min. Subsequently, a standard solution of KOH-isopropanol (0.005 mol·L-1) was prepared as a titration solution. During conductivity titration, the conductivity read by the conductivity titrator was recorded. The curve of conductivity against the titration volume is obtained. The contents of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups were calculated using the following formula [35]:
(1)
r =
V B
-
V A
V A

Here, r is the ratio of hydroxyl to carboxyl (–OH/–COOH). VA and VB are the value of the titrimetric volume for equivalence points (intersections of the tangents) of carboxyl and hydroxyl, respectively.

The E. coli was selected for antibacterial activity testing. It was done by colony forming units (CFUs) counting method and testing the optical density at a wavelength of 600 nm (OD600) through UV–vis spectroscopy. Firstly, the effect of VCDs on E. coli was explored by the CFUs counting method. Centrifuge the bacteria during the exponential growth phase, discard the suspension, and resuspend the bacteria with phosphate buffer (PBS 1x). Then, the bacteria solutions were diluted 10,000 times and 10 μL of diluted bacteria solutions is taken and coated on a broth agar plate with different concentrations of VCDs-1, VCDs-2, VCDs-3 and VCDs-4, respectively. Incubated these plates in an incubator in the dark at 37 ℃ for 24 h and counted the CFUs of E. coli using a colony counter. In another experiment, the same amount of bacteria suspension was dispersed in 5 mL LB medium with different concentrations (0, 200, 400, 600, 800, 1000 μg·mL−1) of VCDs-1, VCDs-2, VCDs-3 and VCDs-4, respectively. The bacteria were incubated in the microbiological incubator (37 ℃, 180 rpm) for 5 h. OD600 of the samples treated with different concentrations of VCDs was determined by UV–vis spectroscopy to monitor the viability of bacteria. The formula for calculating the bacterial viability and antibacterial rate are as follows:
(2)
B a c t e r i a l
v i a b i l i t y
( % )
=
O D
600
′
O D
600
× 100 %
(3)
A n t i b a c t e r i a l
r a t e
( % )
=
( 1 -
O D
600
′
O D
600
)
× 100 %

Where
O D
600
′
and
O D
600
are the values of the bacteria treated with different concentrations of VCDs and the bacteria treat without VCDs, respectively.

The morphology of the E. coli was examined by SEM. First, the bacteria were incubated with VCDs-1, VCDs-2, VCDs-3 and VCDs-4 (200 μg·mL−1) at 37 ℃ for 5 h. Then, these bacterial cells were collected by centrifugation (5000 rpm, 3 min) and fixed with 2.5% glutaraldehyde for 6 h. After fixed, the cells were washed by PBS (0.01 mol/L pH = 7.00) to remove excess glutaraldehyde, and the cells were successively dehydrated by ethanol solution with different volume fractions (30%, 50%, 70%, 85%, 95%, and 100%) for 10 min. Finally, bacteria solutions were dripped and dried on the silicon wafers at 25 ℃. Gold spraying was used for improving sample conductivity before SEM measurement.

The ROS content of bacteria treated without and with VCDs-1, VCDs-2, VCDs-3 and VCDs-4 (200 μg·mL−1) were detected using a ROS assay kit, which is a type of test for ROS detection using the fluorescent probe DCFH-DA [36,37]. DCFH-DA itself has no fluorescence and can freely cross the cell membrane. After entering the cell, it can be hydrolyzed by intracellular esterase to generate DCFH. Intracellular ROS can oxidize nonfluorescent DCFH to generate fluorescent DCF. Detecting the fluorescence of DCF could obtain the level of intracellular ROS. The bacteria (2 mL) were incubated with DCFH-DA (10 μM) at 37 ℃ for 40 min and washed three times by PBS (0.01 mol/L pH = 7.00) to remove DCFH-DA which not entered the bacteria. Then, the bacteria were resuspended in 1 mL PBS. The fluorescence of the bacteria was obtained by a microplate reader (λex = 488 nm and λem = 525 nm).
E.coli were treated with VCDs-1, VCDs-2, VCDs-3, and VCDs-4 for 40 min, respectively. Then, the bacteria are washed and resuspended by PBS for imaging by a CLSM.

The bacterial viability of E. coli and cell viability of 293T treated with different concentrations of VCDs were detected in triplicate. And the results were presented as mean ± standard deviation (SD). The experimental data were statistically analyzed using the Independent-Samples T-test of GraphPad Prism 5. To assess significant differences, probability thresholds of **P < 0.01 and ***P < 0.001 were employed.

Series of VCDs, called VCDs-1 to VCDs-4 for short, were obtained by one-step electrochemical oxidation method from l-ascorbic acid with different reaction times (3 d, 7 d, 11 d and 15 d). The schematic diagram of the preparation process of VCDs is shown in Fig. 1
(a). The morphology and particle size distribution of VCDs were obtained using TEM images and DLS spectra. Fig. 1 b-1e are the TEM images of VCDs-1, VCDs-2, VCDs-3 and VCDs-4, exhibiting that VCDs are typical sphere-like morphology and well dispersed. From the TEM images, their average sizes are 1.27, 2.92 nm, 3.56 nm, and 4.41 nm, respectively. Fig. 1 f-1i show the DLS analysis of four VCDs, exhibiting that the average sizes of VCDs-1, VCDs-2, VCDs-3, and VCDs-4 are 1.40 nm, 4.25 nm, 4.86 nm, and 7.19 nm, respectively. Although the particle size distribution of DLS for VCDs is slightly greater than those of the distribution from the TEM images, both the results from the TEM images and DLS analysis show an increase in size from VCDs-1 to VCDs-4. The above results reveal that the average size of VCDs increases with the extension of reaction time.

The elemental composition, functional groups and structures of VCDs were analyzed by XPS, UV–Vis and FT-IR spectra. The full scan XPS spectra of VCDs are shown in Fig. S1 , which reveal that VCDs are composed of C and O elements and the corresponding atom ratios (Fig. 2
a) are 59.83% and 40.17% (VCDs-1), 59.95% and 40.05% (VCDs-2), 54.59% and 45.41% (VCDs-3), 55.27% and 44.73% (VCDs-4), respectively. There is only little change in their elemental contents for these VCDs. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1s exhibits three main peaks shown in Fig. 2 b. The three peaks located at 284.80 eV, 286.80 eV and 289.03 eV for the VCDs correspond to CC, CO and CO bonds, respectively [24,38,39]. To compare the changes in composition of these VCDs, the peak areas were calculated. In Fig. S2 , from VCDs-1 to VCDs-4, the proportion of CC is decreasing with the increase of CO, and the proportion of CO almost has no change. The change in compositions illustrates that the surface functional groups of VCDs are gradually oxidized while the size increase during the electrolysis process.

The UV–Vis absorption spectra of l-ascorbic acid and VCDs are shown in Fig. S3 . The UV–Vis spectrum of l-ascorbic acid displays an absorption band located at 246 nm, which is corresponded to the electron transition of π-π* [23,40]. Compared to l-ascorbic acid, VCDs have a new absorption peak at 296 nm, which is assigned to n-π* transitions. The FT-IR spectra of raw material l-ascorbic acid (black line), VCDs-1 (red line), VCDs-2 (blue line), VCDs-3 (green line) and VCDs-4 (purple line) are shown in Fig. 2 c. The same adsorption peaks at similar locations indicates that they have similar functional groups. The peak at 3406.1 cm−1 is corresponded to the stretching vibration of OH bond. The two peaks at 1735.8 cm−1 and 1631.7 cm−1 are assigned to CO and CC bond vibrations [41]. The peak at 1081 cm−1 is attributed to the symmetric stretching vibrations of COC bond. These all demonstrate that VCDs are rich in hydrophilic surface functional groups such as –OH and –COOH, which make them show good water solubility. In addition, from the FT-IR spectra, the ratio of CO to CC is gradually increasing from l-ascorbic acid to VCDs-1 and then to VCDs-4 (Table S1). The results also indicate that the electrolysis process of VCDs is an oxidation process. The zeta potentials of VCDs-1, VCDs-2, VCDs-3 and VCDs-4 are shown in Table S2, which shows they have similar zeta potentials.

Next, the conductivity titration was employed to determine the relative content of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the surfaces of the VCDs. The change curves of conductivity against the titration volume of VCDs-1, VCDs-2, VCDs-3 and VCDs-4 are shown in Fig. S4 . The relative ratios of hydroxyl and carboxyl for VCDs-1, VCDs-2, VCDs-3 and VCDs-4 (Fig. 2 d) are about 5.64:1, 5.30:1, 3.99:1 and 2.28:1, respectively. The ratio of –OH/–COOH is gradually decreasing with the increase of reaction time, indicating that –OH was continuously oxidized to –COOH, which is consistent with the results of FT-IR and XPS results. All of these indicate that in the process of VCDs electrolysis, the degree of oxidation also gradually increases while the particle size is increasing.

The cytotoxicity of four VCDs in vitro was tested using a 293T cell line, and the cell viability was detected by CCK-8. As shown in Fig. S5 , even though the concentration of VCDs in the medium is as high as 800 μg·mL−1, there are still around 85 % of 293 T cells survive. These data demonstrate that the VCDs have low toxicity and good biocompatibility.

As the discussed above, four kinds of VCDs with different sizes and oxidation degrees were prepared with different electrolysis times, all of which exhibit good water solubility and biocompatibility. In subsequent studies, the antibacterial activity of these VCDs was explored using the most common gram-negative bacteria, E. coli, as a model. First, the CFUs counting method was used to study the antibacterial behavior among the four VCDs, equal amounts of bacteria suspension were treated with the same concentration (400 μg·mL−1) of VCDs-1, VCDs-2, VCDs-3 and VCDs-4 at 37 ℃ for 24 h in an incubator. As shown in Fig. 3
a, the CFUs of E. coli treated with VCDs-1, VCDs-2, VCDs-3 and VCDs-4 decreased noticeably within 24 h compared with the control group. The CFUs of that experimental group decreased by 38.6%, 61.6%, 49.2% and 45.1%, respectively (Fig. 3 b). The antibacterial activity of them shows VCDs-2 > VCDs-3 > VCDs-4 > VCDs-1. VCDs-2 (average size: 2.92 nm) have the superior antibacterial effect in these VCDs. Then, the OD600 obtained by UV–vis spectroscopy was used to further evaluate the antibacterial activity of the four VCDs. As shown in Fig. 3 c, the bacterial viability was measured by incubating the bacteria treated with VCDs-1, VCDs-2, VCDs-3 and VCDs-4 in LB liquid medium at 37 ℃ for 5 h. The results show that the bacterial viability reduces to different degrees, and the VCDs-1, VCDs-2, VCDs-3 and VCDs-4 were reduced by 30.5%, 92.5%, 82.3% and 52.7%, respectively. The antibacterial activity is also shown VCDs-2 > VCDs-3 > VCDs-4 > VCDs-1, and the results consistent of the data obtained by the CFUs counting method. For VCDs-2, VCDs-3 and VCDs-4, their antibacterial activity gradually weakens as the particle size increases. The smaller VCDs-2, in these particles have the strongest antibacterial activity compared to the other size. However, for VCDs-1, it has the smallest size and the weakest antibacterial activity. In this case, we conclude that VCDs have size-dependent antimicrobial properties. When the size is large enough, the antimicrobial properties decrease with the increase of size. When the size is extremely small, its antibacterial properties are the weakest.

Next, since VCDs-2 have the best antibacterial properties, further studies have been carried out with VCDs-2 as an example. The tests were also performed on E. coli treated with different concentrations (200, 400, 600, 800, and 1000 μg·mL−1) of VCDs-2 by CFUs counting method and testing the OD600 through UV–vis spectroscopy. The antibacterial activity of VCDs-2 shows concentration-dependence, with the concentration of VCDs-2 gradually increasing, the antibacterial rate of the VCDs-2 gradually increases, shown in Fig. 4
a and 4b. In addition, the antibacterial properties of raw material l-ascorbic acid and VCDs-2 were compared by testing the OD600 through UV–vis spectroscopy (Fig. 4 b). The antibacterial properties of l-ascorbic acid raw materials were very weak, but after the formation of VCDs-2 by electrolysis, the antibacterial properties were greatly improved. When the concentration of VCDs-2 is 400 μg·mL−1, the antibacterial rate reaches more than 92%, which is almost completely antibacterial. These results indicate that the obtained VCDs have concentration-dependent antibacterial properties and their antibacterial activity is far greater than that of raw material l-ascorbic acid.

For the CDs we obtained, we could control the synthesis process, crystallinity, the surface functional groups, oxidation degree, morphology, size and surface electricity of the CDs. Finally, to verify whether the different antibacterial properties of VCDs are affected by oxidation degrees, the CDs with the same size but different oxidation degrees were obtained by oxidation of VCDs-2, and the performance test was carried out. As shown in Fig. 4 c, before and after oxygenation (12d, 2d is an interval), the antibacterial properties are almost unchanged. However, the antibacterial properties of the obtained VCDs with different sizes (VCDs-1, VCDs-2, VCDs-3 and VCDs-4) have a significant difference in their antibacterial activity. Therefore, it is shown that size is the main factor in the antibacterial performance of these VCDs. In summary, the obtained VCDs with different sizes have size- and concentration-dependent antibacterial performance. The oxidation degrees of VCDs have no effects on the antibacterial performance.

To further understand the size-dependent antibacterial behavior of VCDs against E. coli, the following antibacterial mechanism of VCDs was explored. The ROS contents of the bacteria treated with VCDs (VCDs-1 ∼ VCDs-4) were measured. Endogenous ROS levels are the most important trigger and apoptosis effector molecules that induce apoptosis. As shown in Fig. 5
a, compared with the control group, the content of the VCDs (VCDs-1 ∼ VCDs-4) groups increase by 0.5%, 41.7%, 34.9% and 8.3%, respectively. The corresponding semi-quantitative analysis of ROS content analyzed by CLSM are shown in Figure S9 . The addition of VCDs would prompt the bacteria to produce excess ROS, which would destroy the cell wall of E. coli and inhibit the growth of bacteria [42,43]. It is worth mentioning that the VCDs-2 could increase the ROS content more observably than other VCDs of different sizes. From the antibacterial test, VCDs-2 have superior antibacterial activity than VCDs-1, VCDs-3 and VCDs-4, which is consistent with the ROS results. Further, the morphologies of bacteria treated with different sizes of VCDs were analyzed by SEM. Fig. 5 b and Fig. 5 c are the SEM images of E. coli treated without or with VCDs-2. The cell walls of E. coli cells for the control group are smooth and clear, while that of cells treated with VCDs-2 are wrinkled and damaged [24,30]. Damaged cell walls resulted in damage to intracellular structures, leakage of cellular contents, and bacteria die finally. SEM of E. coli treated with the VCDs-1, VCDs-3 and VCDs-4 are shown in Fig. S6 . Compared with the bacteria treated with these VCDs, the VCDs-2 group damage to the cell wall more.

Based on the ROS and SEM results, we speculate that the main reason for the size-dependent antibacterial activity for the VCDs is the different uptake of bacteria towards the different sizes of VCDs. Different uptake to the VCDs makes the bacteria generate different content of ROS and varying degrees of destruction for the bacteria cell walls [44–46]. Next, the CLSM was used for demonstrating the absorption capacity of bacteria to VCDs with different sizes. As shown the Fig. S7 , the VCDs (VCDs-1 ∼ VCDs-4) have similar fluorescence properties. Therefore, the fluorescence intensity from the VCDs could demonstrate the uptake capacity of bacteria to these CDs. As shown in Fig. 5 d-e and Fig. S8 , all the VCDs could be absorbed by E. coli, but the small size CDs of VCDs-1 and VCDs-2 are more easily entered into the E. coli. However, although the VCDs-1 is easily absorbed by E. coli, it does not enable the bacteria to produce large amounts of ROS.

As shown in the Fig. 6 , the different size of the VCDs in this work (average size: 1.27, 2.92, 3.56 and 4.41 nm) have size-dependent antibacterial activity toward E. coli. Smaller size of VCDs could more easily absorbed by the bacteria via free diffusion, but the too small VCDs (VCDs-1) would not stimulate the bacteria to produce excess ROS. On the contrary, the bigger VCDs (within the range of 2–5 nm) have low absorbability toward the bacteria with the increase of their sizes, whereas they are more likely to stimulate E. coli producing ROS. On account of the absorbability of VCDs and the excess ROS stimulated by VCDs, VCDs-2 with suitable size (average size: 2.92 nm) have the optimal antibacterial activity. Hence, the reasons for the size-dependent antibacterial activity for the VCDs are the synergy of the absorption capacity of bacteria to VCDs and the ROS stimulated by VCDs.

In this work, four kinds of VCDs with different sizes (VCDs-1 ∼ VCDs-4, average size: 1.27, 2.92, 3.56 and 4.41 nm) were fabricated by one-step electrochemical oxidation method from the l-ascorbic acid at different reaction times. They have the same chemical composition, but different sizes and different degrees of oxidation. Results show that the size is the key factor for the antimicrobial properties of VCDs, but there was non-linear relation between size and antibacterial properties. The size-dependent antibacterial activity of the VCDs is due to the synergy of the absorption capacity of bacteria to VCDs and the ROS stimulated by VCDs. Suitable size of the VCDs would have superior antibacterial ability. Moreover, the antibacterial mechanism was explored by ROS, SEM and CLSM and so on, demonstrating suitable size of VCDs could induce more cellular oxidative stress caused by the generation of ROS, damage the cell wall, and inhibit the growth and reproduction of bacteria. This work reveals the antibacterial mechanism of the CDs, unlocks a deeper understanding of CDs size-antibacterial activity relationships, and provide a direction for the design of antimicrobials with high-efficiency.

Xin Du: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Mengling Zhang: Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Visualization. Yurong Ma: Visualization, Investigation. Xiting Wang: Software, Validation. Yang Liu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Hui Huang: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Zhenhui Kang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2022.12.025.

ce:section.ce:para:
